# `sesam status` reports how the revealed plaintext compares to the sealed state.
# --ignore-unmanaged keeps these assertions focused on sesam-tracked secrets;
# the unmanaged-file classification is covered by repo unit tests.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

# Init creates README.md, registers it as a secret, and seals it.
exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# Fresh out of init the revealed README matches its sealed object.
exec sesam status --ignore-unmanaged --json
stdout 'README.md'
stdout '"same_content"'

# Modify the revealed secret without re-sealing; status must flag the drift.
cp modified_readme.md README.md
exec sesam status --ignore-unmanaged --json
stdout '"diff_content"'

# Table output (no --json) still names the secret.
exec sesam status --ignore-unmanaged
stdout 'README.md'

# --diff shells out to git to show old (sealed) vs new (revealed) content.
exec sesam status --ignore-unmanaged --diff
stdout 'modified readme'

# Removing the revealed plaintext entirely reports it as unrevealed.
rm README.md
exec sesam status --ignore-unmanaged --json
stdout '"unrevealed"'

-- modified_readme.md --
# modified readme

This is the new content after the change.
